attributes.stem:
A 40-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a four-month history of progressive lower abdominal pain and bloating. She previously had a regular menstrual cycle, however in the last four months they have become irregular. Her bowel habits are normal, and she has no nausea or vomiting. She has a past medical history of type 2 diabetes for which she takes metformin. She smokes 18 cigarettes per day for the last 14 years and has two glasses of wine three nights a week.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her BMI is 28 kg/m2. The abdominal and pelvic examinations are unremarkable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial investigation?
attributes.explanation:
Abnormal uterine bleeding can be due to early onset menopause, the presence of abdominal pain and bloating is not typical of this presentation. Other causes that should be considered include cervical cancer, uterine fibroids, endometrial carcinoma and carcinoma of the ovary. Each of these conditions can present with abnormal uterine bleeding, however the symptom of bloating is concerning for ovarian cancer, which often presents with non-specific symptoms leading to late diagnoses. In this scenario, it is important to ensure that pregnancy is excluded prior to proceeding to further investigation, and a cervical co-test should be collected.

The first investigation should be a transvaginal pelvic ultrasound (TVUSS) to assess for endometrial thickness, areas of abnormal endometrium or myometrium and adnexal masses. This will determine the focus for any further investigations including biopsies. Even if there is no abnormality identified on the TVUSS, the patient may still be referred to a gynaecologist for consideration of a hysteroscopy and an endometrial biopsy if abnormal bleeding continues. Endometrial sampling will identify if endometrial hyperplasia or cancer is present, however, given the patient's additional symptoms of bloating and abdominal pain, a TVUSS is important to exclude adnexal pathology prior to further assessment

Serum cancer antigen 125 (Ca125) is a tumour marker that can be elevated in ovarian cancer. However, it is not specific to ovarian cancer and can be elevated in many other conditions including pregnancy, pelvic infection, endometriosis and fibroids. When an ovarian mass is identified, Ca125 can be used to calculate the risk of malignancy index. However, it can be unreliable in premenopausal women so a treating specialist may choose to repeat the test 4-6 weeks after it is initially done to guide . It should not be used as a screening test for ovarian cancer, or as an initial investigation of symptoms that may suggest ovarian cancer.

CT of the pelvis is less sensitive for the detection of endometrial abnormalities or adnexal masses and should not be used in the evaluation of abnormal uterine bleeding. In cases where there is confirmed diagnosis of cancer, a CT scan may be ordered in order to assess the extent of disease and to detect any lymph node involvement or metastasis.

A hysterosalpingogram is a study in which contrast dye is inserted into the uterus, allowing for the assessment of the patency of the endometrial cavity and fallopian tubes. It does not allow for assessment of the endometrial thickness or assess for adnexal masses so will not be useful in this scenario.
learningPoints: Gynaecological cancers should always be considered in perimenopausal women with atypical symptoms.
Transvaginal ultrasound is the most appropriate initial investigation for perimenopausal women with bloating, abdominal pain and irregular bleeding.
Serum cancer antigen 125 testing should be done in the presence of an ovarian mass to calculate the Risk of Malignancy Index and guide further investigation.
references: ‌Yeoh M. Investigation and management of an ovarian mass. AJGP [Internet]. 2015 Feb [cited 2024 Aug 20];44(1-2):48–52. Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/getattachment/6de4486e-d524-4c88-9d97-9014c99f0254/Investigation-and-management-of-an-ovarian-mass.aspx
Lheureux S, Gourley C, Vergote I, et al. Epithelial ovarian cancer. Lancet 2019; 393: 1240–53

The Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Radiologists and Cancer Australia. Abnormal Vaginal Bleeding: A diagnostic guide for General Practitioners and Gynaecologists. . [Updated 2011 February]. Available at: https://www.canceraustralia.gov.au/sites/default/files/publications/ncgc-vaginal-bleeding-flowcharts-march-20111_504af02038614.pdf
